title: Tracking visible pulsed laser annealing of Hf0.5Zr0.5O2 heterostructures with in situ transmission electron microscopy
authors: Aida Amini, Shruti Verma, Katharina Kohlmann, Sebastian Obernberger, Jean-Christof Lamanque, Andreas Rüdiger, Kenneth R. Beyerlein
abstract: Laser annealing offers a promising route to back end of the line fabrication of ferroelectric thin film transistors based on hafnium-zirconium oxide (HZO). Due to the wide band gap of this material, previous reports have studied the crystallization of HZO using ultraviolet or infrared light. In contrast, we monitor its crystallization in a Si3N4/TiN/Hf0.5Zr0.5O2 thin film heterostructure upon irradiation with visible nanosecond laser pulses. This geometry mimics the structure of CMOS devices and harnesses the absorption of TiN in the visible regime to generate the heat necessary for the transformation. Through a series of local in situ measurements using a modified transmission electron microscope, we quantify the relationship between the HZO film thickness, critical laser energy density and the ferroelectric HZO phase fraction, finding a sharp threshold behavior in the laser pulse energy necessary to crystallize HZO. The optimal condition of irradiating an 8-nm HZO film with a single laser pulse with an energy density of 177 mJ/cm2 is found to produce 86% of the ferroelectric orthorhombic phase. Heat transfer dynamics within the heterostructure during laser annealing are revealed by finite element simulations, where the partial melting of the silicon nitride substrate is found to play an important role limiting the temperature to 1900 °C. This finding as well as the observed laser pulse energy threshold behavior support a kinetic crystallization pathway involving the tetragonal phase. More generally, these findings show how laser-driven phase engineering can lead to scalable design and enhanced performance of ferroelectric materials in advanced electronic applications.
summary: This paper investigates how visible pulsed laser annealing can be used to control the phase formation in HZO thin films. By using in situ TEM, the researchers tracked the crystallization process in real-time. They found that an 8-nm thick film reaches a high ferroelectric phase fraction when pulsed with a specific energy density. The study also reveals that the melting of the substrate helps regulate the peak temperature, supporting a specific kinetic pathway for phase transformation. This work provides a roadmap for using laser-driven engineering to create high-performance ferroelectric materials for advanced electronics.
main_problem: Understanding the crystallization dynamics and phase evolution of Hf0.5Zr0.5O2 (HZO) thin films during pulsed laser annealing to maximize the ferroelectric orthorhombic phase.
main_result: The study identified an optimal 8-nm HZO thickness and a single-shot laser energy density of 177 mJ/cm2 that produces an 86% ferroelectric orthorhombic phase fraction, following a kinetic crystallization pathway.
method: In situ transmission electron microscopy (TEM) combined with nanosecond pulsed visible laser irradiation and finite element simulations to model heat transfer.
model_or_system: A Si3N4/TiN/Hf0.5Zr0.5O2 thin-film heterostructure designed to mimic CMOS device structures, using TiN as a visible-light absorbing heat source.
key_observables: HZO crystallinity, phase fraction (orthorhombic, tetragonal, and monoclinic), and SAED ring patterns.
important_parameters: Laser energy density (fluence), HZO film thickness (7, 8, and 15 nm), heating rates (up to 3e11 C/s), and peak temperatures (up to 1900 C).
main_assumptions: The heat transfer model assumes a Gaussian spatial and flat-top temporal heat source and incorporates the partial melting of the Si3N4 substrate.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows SAED images and TEM boundaries of crystalline/non-crystalline regions; Figure 2 plots crystallization threshold fluence vs. thickness; Figure 3 shows SAED radial distribution intensity profiles and phase models; Figure 6 shows SAED radial distribution profiles.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of HZO phase stabilization, describes the in situ TEM/laser experimental setup, presents quantitative phase analysis of SAED patterns, discusses thickness-dependent threshold behavior, provides thermal modeling via finite element simulations, and concludes with implications for scalable electronic applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. SAED images of 8-nm-thick HZO heterostructure (a) before, (b) after laser titration PLA at 177 mJ/cm2, and (c) after single-shot PLA at 153 mJ/cm2. (d) TEM image of the HZO structure showing the boundary of the crystallized region as indicated on the image. SAED pattern of 15- nm-thick HZO heterostructure (e) before and (f) after laser titration PLA at 183 mJ/cm2.
Figure 2. Threshold laser fluence for HZO film crystallization as a function of HZO film thickness and PLA experiment type.

Figure 3. Measured SAED radial distribution intensity profiles and best-fit model patterns are shown for the (a) 7-nm (𝜒2 = 0.032), (b) 8-nm (𝜒2 = 0.021), (c) 15-nm (𝜒2 = 0.060) HZO heterostructure samples. As-deposited and annealed profiles correspond to measurements made on the same area of the sample before and after laser intensity titration PLA experiments. Model patterns are associated with the QPA model consisting of oIV-HZO, m-HZO and TiN phases that was found to best fit annealed patterns. (d) The positions of dominant peaks from each phase used in the model are indicated by vertical lines.

Figure 4. (a) Temperature evolution of laser-induced heating in the 8-nm HZO heterostructure probed at different depths. Inset shows the location of the probes. (b) Long-time evolution of temperature showing beginning of cooling.
